videos.title: Red Bull 400: The hardest 400m Run in Europe
videos.description:
Climb to more adventures here: http://win.gs/1aXUVRM
Click CC for Subtitles!
On a beautiful and sunny Saturday, 164 runners from 9 countries competed against each other trying to conquer the famous ski jump in Planica. The hardest 400 m run was won by the Turk Arslan Ahmet in the men category (5:02:53), and by the Austrian Teresa Stadlober in the women category (6:46:44). The best Slovene runners were Petra Mikloša and her brother Matjaž, both placed second. 

V lepem vremenu, sprva tudi na toplem soncu, se je v Planici 156 tekačev in tekačič iz 9 držav pomerilo v osvajanju velikanke. Najtežji tek na 400 metrov je med moškimi dobil Turek Arslan Ahmet, pri ženskah pa Avstrijka Teresa Stadlober. Najboljša Slovenca sta bila Petra Mikloša pri dekletih in njen brat Matjaž pri fantih, oba na drugem mestu. Tek je potekal po slavni smučarski letalnici navkreber, od dna doskočišča, pa vse do vrha zaleta.

http://www.redbull.si/cs/Satellite/sl_SI/Red-Bull-400---Home/001243250192052
___________________________________________________________

Experience the world of Red Bull like you have never seen it before. With the best action sports clips on the web and original series, prepare for your "stoke factor" to be at an all time high.

Red Bull on Facebook: http://win.gs/redbullfb

Red Bull on Twitter: http://win.gs/redbulltwitter

Subscribe to Red Bull on Youtube: http://www.youtube.com/subscription_center?add_user=redbull

Sign up for our Newsletter here: http://win.gs/RedBullNewsletter

videos.title: COROS PACE 4 In-Depth Review // The Best Budget Sportswatch Just Got Better
videos.description:
Full review of the COROS Pace 4 sports watch including GPS and heart rate accuracy, sports features, health features, and more. Get the COROS Pace 4 here: https://geni.us/COROSPACE4 (affiliate link that helps support the channel - thanks!)

Related videos:
COROS Pace Pro Review: https://youtu.be/6iUYgVxdFZk?si=b4P0MLI7xDyzws4_
COROS Pace 3 Review: https://youtu.be/koI9lk5HHSs?si=ZfBvusmFqMjGzOe9
COROS Apex 4 Review: https://youtu.be/F2bORMcdGT0?si=x6jmZrBe53RMM7jF

/// Gear I Use ///
Watch: https://geni.us/zvdFe
Bike GPS: https://geni.us/YPEv
Chest Heart Rate Strap: https://geni.us/3b2gj
Arm Heart Rate Strap: https://geni.us/BSLAWK
Headphones: https://geni.us/B2is
Affiliate links that help support the channel without any additional cost to you :)

/// Connect & Updates ///
Twitter: https://twitter.com/GetDesFit
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/getdesfit/
Strava: https://www.strava.com/athletes/DesFit
Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/GetDesFit

Videos may contain affiliate links which can help support and grow this channel with no extra cost to you. Thanks for your continued support.
videos.transcript:
So, when it comes to feature-p packed
lightweight sports watches, Corsa's Pace
series have always been a great choice,
offering plenty of sports features in a
small, budget friendly package. And with
this new Pace 4, so they've updated it
with some new hardware. It now has an
AMOLED display. It still gets decent
battery life. And maybe most
importantly, they've kept the price
pretty low. So, I've been testing the
new Pace 4 for a bit now. And in this
video, we're going to talk about the new
hardware, the realworld battery life
that you could get, a new microphone
that they've added, and what you could
do with it, and lots of accuracy
testing, of course, for both GPS as well
as heart rate. And I'll also talk about
if this watch is worth the upgrade if
you already own a previous Pace series
watch. Oh, and one more thing, too, is
that I also have some details on some
updated pricing for the Pace Pro, as
well as a previous Pace 3. So, stay
tuned for that in just a bit. So, the
new Pace 4 is of course his new
entry-level watch, but it comes with a
surprising amount of features
considering the price and it's coming in
at $249. So, just a slight increase from
the Pace 3, which was $229 when it first
came out. But in the whole landscape of
sports watches, it's a really reasonable
price considering what's inside. So,
other than price, I think another big
reason people look at course as Pace
Series is for size and weight. Where
these are small, lightweight watches,
and this one in particular has a 43 mm
case. And this is how it looks on my 185
mm circumference wrist. So, these are
designed to be incredibly comfortable,
whether that's going to be for running,
regular daily use, and even sleeping
with them as well. A lot of us want a
super capable sports watch, but we don't
necessarily want the bulk of a larger
watch. And that's really one of the
highlights of the Pace series where this
is incredibly light and thin, where this
is just a little bit under 40 g with a
silicone band. And they also have a
nylon band option planned, but it wasn't
quite ready at the time of this filming,
but should be available just a little
bit after this video is published, I
believe. And the claim weight with the
nylon band is going to be around 32 g.
Either way, whether it's the silicone
band or the nylon band, this is the kind
of watch where it's small and light
enough that you kind of tend to forget
that you're wearing it. But arguably,
the biggest change they made with the
Pace 4 is moving to an AMOLED display
versus the memory pixel display that
they've had before. So there's
advantages and disadvantages with both
of these kinds of displays where memory
and pixel displays consume less power
than AMOLED. Then there's going to be
readability where AMOLED displays are
just like your smartphone where these
have tons of colors, tons of contrast,
and they also get really bright with
memory and pixel displays. So these
aren't as vibrant and also have less
pixels. However, in certain outdoor
conditions where the sun is directly
hitting these displays, they can
sometimes be more visible in those
scenarios. But the Pace 4 has a peak
brightness of 1500 nits, which I found
to be plenty bright enough to see
outside. So there's really not going to
be any issues with either of these in
sunny conditions. But when it comes to
cloudy or overcast conditions, so this
is going to be a little bit different
where AMOLED displays actually may do a
little bit better. And they have three
different brightness levels to choose
from here. And for daily use, I usually
prefer the lowest brightness level on
most AMOLED watches and that's what I
used on the Pace 4. However, for outdoor
use, that's where I may crank them up a
bit just depending on the conditions.
Oh, and really quick, do me a little
favor and hit that like button down
below. Okay, back to the video.
Realistically though, either kind of
display is going to be fine outside, but
companies have been moving to AMOLED
just due to the fact that they simply
look better when it comes to color,
contrast, and pixels. So, I'm not
necessarily surprised to see Coors go
this route. However, the trade-off is
going to be battery life. So, with the
Pace 4, so this still delivers pretty
decent battery life overall. And battery
life will differ depending on the
settings that you use. And it primarily
comes down to if you want to use the
always on display mode or not. So if you
don't use the always on display mode,
what happens here is that the display
turns off when you're not interacting
with the watch and then it wakes up when
you turn your wrist or press a button.
With this kind of mode, they advertise
up to 19 days of battery life. However,
if you're going to be using the always
on display mode, so what happens here is
that the display dims to a lower
brightness when you're not interacting
with it. So you can still see the time
or your workout data at a glance without
having to turn your wrist. And with the
always on display mode, they advertise
up to six days. And I think that
estimate is about right where I've been
getting around five days out of it
before having to recharge it. And I have
been doing some long outdoor rides
recently. And I also have been using the
real-time heart rate recording setting
as well as sleep tracking. One thing I
note though is that I was using the
lowest brightness setting for all my
testing, which is what I tend to prefer,
but the battery life will probably drop
if you use a higher brightness level.
And then when it comes to outdoor
activity tracking, so they advertise
anywhere from 24 hours in their maximum
accuracy dual frequency mode while using
the always on display all the way up to
41 hours if you turn the always on
display off in their single band mode.
And for my testing, so I was using the
always on display in the lowest
brightness level. And when I was not
using a route, it actually exceeded
their claims. And when I was using a
route, it was just slightly under. So
I'd say those numbers are pretty darn
solid. Again though, if you are going to
be using a higher brightness level, I'd
expect those to drop a bit. So to get
around the Pace 4, it has a touchcreen
along with three physical buttons. So
the upper right hand button is both a
rotating dial as well as a enter button
and then the lower right hand button. So
this is going to be a back button and
then the lower leftand corner, this is
their new action button. And I'm really
liking Cororus's new thinking on this
additional button here where for outdoor
activities, you can actually use this
button to switch back and forth between
the map page where instead of being a
separate data page, you'd actually just
switch back and forth from the map page
from any screen. But you can also
customize this to do other functions
like their new voice pin feature, which
we'll talk about in just one second. But
they also do allow you to customize the
behavior of both the action button and
the back button to do different things.
Like I don't usually mark laps, so then
I can actually swap the lap button
behavior to something else like taking a
voice pin. And that brings us to another
hardware upgrade where this now has a
microphone. And the use cases for this
are kind of interesting where it's not
for taking calls or anything like that.
So, the microphone on this is more for
taking voice notes. Whether that's for
logging something performance or
nutrition related during your activity,
like tracking how many carbs that you're
taking every hour, or maybe don't ever
buy that mashed potato flavored gel ever
again, or even just random thoughts that
you may have during your run. Are donuts
or waffles better? Discuss. I mean, I
think a lot of us come up with some
pretty great ideas when we're out in a
run or a ride. And the nice thing about
all these notes is that there's even
automatic transcription that happens in
the app. And these notes are all just
synced over when you save your activity.
Now, even though the Pace 4 has a
microphone, it doesn't have a speaker
like what's found on their recent Apex
4. So, you can't take calls on the Pace
4. It still makes sounds, though, so it
still has a beeper, but not necessarily
a full-blown speaker. And then when it
comes to health and activity tracking,
so this can track your heart rate, your
calories burned, your steps, it tracks
your sleep, it tracks your heart rate
variability, and it also can track your
stress. Now, it also does have an SPO2
sensor for tracking blood oxygen
saturation levels, but this isn't
currently used for sleep tracking.
rather this is more designed to be used
in high altitude situations. And then
for sport profile, so this is a full-on
multiport watch where it tracks running,
cycling, swimming both in the pool as
well as open water. It also has
multiport and triathlon modes. It has
plenty of gym profiles and a pretty good
selection of outdoor recreation profiles
as well. The ones that you'll get with
their higherend watches are going to be
more on the outdoor recreation side of
things where those get profiles for like
climbing and fishing, like what's going
to be found on their Nomad for instance.
And then when it comes to the data that
the Pace 4 can collect. So when it comes
specifically for running, so other than
the usual stuff that you'd expect like
pace, heart rate, and cadence, the Pace
4 can also collect more advanced running
dynamics directly from the wrist like
stride length, running power, ground
contact time, stride height, and stride
ratio. And then when it comes to broader
training feedback, so it can track your
training load over time. It tracks your
training status. It can also estimate
your recovery time. And then it can also
track your running performance. And it
also has a race predictor for potential
times you could run based on your
running history. And I actually find
these times to be pretty realistic based
on my current running volume. And then
it could also attempt to estimate your
cycling FTP when you use a cycling power
meter. However, this number is quite a
bit higher than my actual cycling FTP,
which is usually right around 280 to 285
watts. And then when it comes to routes
and navigation, so this has breadcrumb
style routing where it can show you a
plot of where you've been on your
outdoor activity where you can use that
to get back to your start location, but
it doesn't have offline maps like
courses, higherend watches. You can
still, however, load in a route file to
follow along, but it just doesn't have
the additional context of the maps,
which gives you an idea of what may be
around you. So, when it comes to
accuracy for tracking outdoor
activities, the Pace 4 comes with a
dualband satellite chipset, just like
their other watches. And with the Pace
4, they have two different modes that
you can choose from here. So, a single
frequency mode that they call their high
mode, and then a dual frequency mode
they call max. And for my testing, I've
been doing a lot of activities lately up
in the hills where there's a lot of
heavy tree cover and a lot of it in some
steep canyons. So, I've been using the
dual frequency mode. However, if you
tend to do activities where you're out
in the wide open where there's not
necessarily anything interfering with a
clear view of the sky, you could
potentially just use the single band
mode. Anyhow, moving on to accuracy. So,
for the total distances collected, rock
solid in all my runs and rides and the
pace for lined up really well with all
the other devices. And for the finer
detail of the GPS tracks themselves, so
like on this gravel ride here, really
solid where there's no wavering or
anything like that, even on some of the
higher speed corners and curves. Now, on
some harder tests, though, so this ride
here was up in the hills with some steep
canyon walls, and the Pace 4 did
extremely well here. There were some
really solid tracks considering these
conditions. And then the Pace 4 also has
a built-in alttimeter for tracking
elevation, gain, and loss. And for
almost all my rides and runs, it was
right in line with all the other
devices, as well as a reference source.
And in this case, it was Straa's
corrected elevation figure. However, on
this rather flat ride that I just had
the other day, interestingly, it
reported quite a bit higher than the
other devices. And if we look at the
elevation plot over time, there's this
kind of odd shakiness that was happening
with the elevation data. There weren't
necessarily any big spikes or shifts,
but I think all that shakiness over the
course of the ride added up to that
higher than expected elevation gain.
Now, moving on to heart rate accuracy.
So, the Pace 4 comes with Chorus's
latest generation heart rate sensor, and
it's a decent sensor for the most part
where for stuff like indoor cycling,
rock solid on this ride where it matched
up perfectly with all the other heart
rate sensors I was using. And then on
this ride here, same deal where it was
good to go. Now, indoor cycling is a
pretty easy activity for heart rate
sensor to get right just because there's
not really much going on. But taking it
outside, so this is where there's going
to be more variables for watch to deal
with like bumps and vibrations from the
road as well as gripping on the
handlebars which can throw off accuracy.
So, for example, on this ride here, on
the majority of the ride, it tracked
well. However, there were a handful of
spikes that happened during the ride
where it got thrown off for some reason
or another. They were momentary for the
most part, but it certainly did have
some spikes in some spots. Now, on this
ride here, so this is a nearly 4 and 1/2
hour long ride with plenty of road and
lots of gravel. And you can see that as
I was ascending, it did a decent job at
tracking. However, on some of these
downhill sections, it started to get
thrown off. And some of these sections
were on some pretty rough gravel where
that can play havoc on heart rate
sensor, and that was certainly the case
here. But you can definitely see that
even more so with a heavier watch like
the Sundto Vertical 2 I was wearing on
my other wrist. But when it comes to
running, pretty decent overall where for
the majority of the run, it was right in
line with the other senses I was
wearing. But it did waver a bit by a few
beats per minute during this section
right here and then a little bit at the
end. And then I also had this trail run
here. And on the ascent, it was
relatively similar to the other sensors
I was wearing. However, on the downhill
section, it appears that I got caught in
a cadence lock just like the Sundto
Vertical 2 did where an optical heart
rate sensor can mistake the rhythm of
your footsteps as heart rate data. And
then finally, when it comes to weight
training and high-intensity interval
training, basically the hardest of all
when it comes to accuracy with an
optical sensor, this is actually a
pretty decent result for a watch. And
I'd also say pretty similar to some
other watches out there where arm curls
in particular seem to pose challenges
for wristbased optical heart rate sensor
where that kind of gripping and arm
movement likely tense the wrist in such
a way that watches really don't like.
For the bench presses and the shoulder
flies I did right here, it did an
overall decent job and also did a great
job at the high intensity intervals at
the end. But if you do want more
accurate heart rate data, you can pair
up an external heart rate strap with the
Pace 4 along with cycling power meters,
smart bike trainers, and some other
sensors. So, it does have that
capability. So, even though the Pace 4
did increase in price by $20, this
continues with Chorus's tradition of
delivering a valuepacked lightweight
sports watch at a great price. It's
simply a really good deal for the money,
and it is extremely competitive with
watches from other brands at this price.
What's really interesting though is that
Chorus is also announcing price drops
will be happening around the middle of
November with both the previous Pace 3
as well as the Pace Pro and it seems
like the Pace Pro just came out. So,
they really are going for blood here
when it comes to competing on price.
However, with the price drop of the Pace
Pro, so that puts this Pace 4 in a tough
spot where for just $50 more, you can
get the Pace Pro that has offline maps
but doesn't have the microphone. The
microphone may be here or there
depending on how you want to use your
watch, but the maps definitely are a big
difference. So, if you're considering
this new Pace 4, well, it's kind of hard
to ignore that Pace Pro at the new
price. However, the Pace Pro is a larger
watch and isn't nearly as felt as the
Pace 4. Again, there's definitely
something to be said about something
that this light and has this many
features. And if you want an even more
in-depth comparison, so I'm going to
have a follow-up video comparing the
Pace 4 with the Pace Pro. So, if you
want to get a notification when that
video comes out, just tap that little
bell icon right next to the subscribe
button down below to get a little ding
when that drops. Anyhow, if you have any
other questions about the Pace 4 I
didn't cover in this video, feel free to
drop those down in the comment section
down below and I'll do my best to get
those answered. But in the meantime,
have fun out there and we will see you
in the next video.

videos.title: Kwak O-tour Beskydy - Stage 3 - Long - EN
videos.description:
This was the last stage of Kwak O-tour Beskydy and the final race of the whole series. It was a long course with several legs, and good route choice played a significant role. The terrain was tough with steep climbs, marshes, and small but deep valleys. It was a joy.

I made one big mistake when attacking the control, one bad route choice and one smaller mistake. Overall I lost quite a bit and placed 16th. The competition was too big and no mistakes were unpunished.

I absolutely loved this series and can't wait to see what's in store for next year!

Chapters:
0:00 - Intro
1:05 - Start
6:14 - Control nr.1
12:10 - Control nr.2
13:42 - Control nr.3
25:08 - Control nr.4
28:00 - Control nr.5
30:42 - Control nr.6
